7 min read
Just now

--

Through the invention of computers, mankind has developed many technologies to get work done faster, more comfortably, and securely. One of the technologies making this possible is programming languages. We have developed tons of programming languages over the years, and as computer systems grow, new ones will continue to be developed. But why do we need this many programming languages?
Fortran was developed by IBM in 1957. This was the first high-level programming language (we will get to that later). Although it lost its hype compared to its early years, it still has niche use cases today.
What about C? According to most people, C is the father of many modern languages. It was released in 1972 and is still one of the most popular programming languages. Many schools still use it in their syllabus as a core pillar.
Yet, we could not stop there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Resource: Evolution of Programming Languages | Tecky AcademyInformal and Formal Languages
Before we dive into programming languages, I want to give a brief explanation of the nature of languages. The languages we use every day to communicate are called informal (natural) languages. Even if we make mistakes such as typos or bad pronunciation, we can ignore those mistakes and still understand each other.
However, this is not acceptable for formal languages. Formal languages are the languages we use to communicate with computers. They cannot tolerate ambiguity. Therefore, we have to be completely clear and precise about what we want to do.
Low, Mid, and High Level Programming Languages
This categorization is all about the closeness between us and the computer. It boils down to the question: “How much should you concern yourself with the computer’s hardware?” The more you have to concern yourself with it, the lower the level.
Low-Level Languages (Machine Code and Assembly Language)
Machine Code tells the computer what to do using binary number systems. Writing it was a nightmare for programmers.
Realizing that words are much easier to remember than numbers, developers created a new language to make their jobs easier. Assembly Language was released about 5 years after Machine Code.
Mid-Level Languages (Rust, C, C++)
Thanks to these languages, you can control both the hardware and software of a computer. You can use human-readable keywords such as for, while, and if, while still accessing hardware directly through pointers.
High-Level Languages (Python, JavaScript, Java, Go)
Here, you have almost zero direct access to the hardware. Managing memory or knowing the exact memory address of your data are no longer your concern. These languages handle such tasks in the background (for instance, using a Garbage Collector as in Java). You just focus on writing code. They are generally much easier to read, write, and learn than lower-level languages.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Resource: Difference between High Level and Low level languages — GeeksforGeeksKey Concepts of Programming Languages
Programming languages have some properties that categorize them.
Name
Names are identifiers that allow us to represent variables, constants,
functions, types, and operations.
Variable
A symbolic name given to a memory location that holds data, allowing developers to store, retrieve, and modify values throughout the program’s execution.
Address
The unique numerical identifier that represents a specific location in the computer’s memory (RAM) where data is stored.
Value
The actual data or content such as a number, text, or boolean stored inside a variable.
Type
Determines how much memory a variable will occupy, the range of values it can hold, and what operations can be performed on those values.
Lifetime
The time interval between when a variable is allocated memory and when that memory is released or deallocated by the system. It is divided into 4 main categories.
Static: Allocated before execution; exists for the entire lifetime of the program. (C, C++, C#, Java, Fortran)
Stack Dynamic: Allocated on the stack when a function is entered and removed upon exit. (C, C++, Java, Rust)
Explicit Heap Dynamic: Explicitly allocated and deallocated by the programmer at runtime. (C, C++, Pascal)
Implicit Heap Dynamic: Automatically allocated on the heap when a value is assigned, managed entirely by the language runtime. (Python, JavaScript, PHP)
Scope
Determines which part of the code can accessible, visible, or changeable from where.
Static (Lexical) Scope: Scope is determined at compile-time based on the physical structure and nesting of the code. (Python, JavaScript, C, Java, Go, Rust)
Dynamic Scope: Scope is determined at runtime based on the execution stack and call chain of functions. (Bash)
Programming Languages Paradigms
Every programming language has its own philosophy. Languages are categorized according to which fields they serve and how they approach solving problems.
Imperative Programming
It focuses on describing to the computer “how to do” the work step-by-step. The state of the program and variables are updated constantly throughout the execution. Assembly Language and Machine Code can be considered pure imperative.
Procedural Programming (C, Fortran, Pascal, COBOL, Go)
It focuses on giving instructions to the computer using procedures or functions. It emerged to solve the “spaghetti code” problem of Assembly Language. Procedural Programming allows us to divide the code into smaller subprograms and handle complex tasks properly.
Object-Oriented Programming (Java, C++, C#, Smalltalk)
This paradigm is built around objects, classes, and methods. Thanks to the core pillars of OOP (Polymorphism, Encapsulation, Inheritance, and Abstraction) it is, in my opinion, the perfect programming paradigm for many complex tasks.
Polymorphism: Allowing a single interface to execute different behaviors.
Encapsulation: Bundling data and hiding internal implementation.
Inheritance: Deriving a new class from an existing parent class.
Abstraction: Showing only essential features, hiding complexity.
Declarative Programming
Instead of step-by-step descriptions, we simply specify what we want to obtain.
Functional Programming (Haskell, Lasp, Erlang)
It is based on mathematical principles. Immutability is its most important feature. Once you define a variable, you cannot change its value later in the code.
Logical Programming (Prolog)
Developers do not write standard functions, algorithms, or step-by-step instructions. Instead, they define facts and rules, and then let the system find answers using queries.
Multi-Paradigm Languages (Python, JavaScript, Rust, C++, TypeScript, Swift, Kotlin)
Many modern languages are not restricted to just one paradigm. Depending on the project requirements, they allow developers to combine multiple approaches seamlessly.
Other Languages
We also have Markup (HTML), Style Sheet (CSS), Query (SQL), and Domain-Specific (JSON) languages. However, to keep this article focused on core programming logic, I will not be diving into them today.
The Popularity of Different Languages
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Resource: Top 10 Programming Languages in 2026Use Cases of Programming Languages
Although you can build almost anything with most languages, every language has its own specific use cases. Computer Science has broken down into many different branches, and each branch has unique demands. Data Science, Artificial Intelligence, Game Development, Mobile Applications… All of them have their own top-tier languages.
Python
Python is peak in terms of Data Science and Artificial Intelligence.Since it has a simple syntax, mathematicians and engineers can benefit from its huge library ecosystem without worrying about complex code blocks, allowing them to focus directly on data and models.
Despite its advantages, Python is a relatively slow language. Being an interpreted language combined with a dynamic typing system slows down its execution speed. Dynamic typing also makes code more prone to runtime errors compared to statically-typed languages. Because of these performance and safety trade-offs, it is rarely chosen as the primary core language for heavy banking systems or real-time financial applications.
C++
It forms the fundamental backbone of Unreal Engine. Because it provides direct access to hardware and memory (RAM), it optimizes gaming performance and enables users to experience gameplay without latency or freezing. However, compared to Python, it is harder to learn and master.
C#
Unity relies heavily on C#. It is often considered the best option for 2D games, and indie developers benefit from it the most due to its accessibility and rapid development capabilities.
Go
Developed by Google to build concurrent and highly scalable systems. The core tools of modern Cloud infrastructure, such as Docker and Terraform, are developed using Go. It is fast and efficient.
Java
Java is a cornerstone of Enterprise Software and Cloud Computing. It is the traditional backbone of backend services in cloud environments. Thanks to the Java Virtual Machine (JVM), it applies the famous “Write once, run anywhere” philosophy. It is highly secure, reliable, and capable of handling massive datasets.
Swift and Kotlin
Swift: Developed by Apple, it offers 100% native compatibility with the iPhone, iPad, and Mac ecosystems.
Kotlin: Recommended by Google for modern Android application development. It works perfect with Java.
JavaScript
Thanks to its event-driven nature, it is one of the best choices for interactive websites and web applications. Its cross-platform ecosystem is truly what keeps the modern Internet alive.
Conclusion
Today, I tried to explain the fundamentals of programming languages, their core paradigms, specific use cases, and current trends. If you want to examine a system that combines Python, Cloud Systems, and Data Engineering, feel free to check out my previous article!
